title: Coworking After the Shakeout
verdict: The survivors are the operators who understood they were in the hospitality business, not the real estate business.
evidence: The coworking sector's post-WeWork consolidation has produced a clear set of winners, and they share a common profile: smaller portfolios, higher revenue per member, and an operational model built around service rather than scale. Operators like Industrious, The Wing's successor concepts, and a handful of regional independents have stabilized occupancy above 85% by emphasizing food and beverage quality, community programming, and design standards that rival boutique hotels. Meanwhile, the legacy flex-space operators that competed on volume and discount pricing continue to struggle with churn rates above 30% annually.
frame: The lesson for building owners is that coworking tenancy is not a single category; it is a spectrum from commodity desk rental to premium hospitality, and the two ends produce radically different outcomes for the host building. An operator that runs a tight, well-designed space with genuine community becomes an amenity that enhances the building's overall positioning. An operator that stuffs desks into open floors and competes on price becomes a liability. Landlords evaluating coworking proposals should be asking about the food program and the events calendar before they ask about the lease structure.
